# Data Spec: Country Data Overview (mock data, frontend-only demo)

> Companion to `design-spec.md` (the spec; where they disagree, the
> spec wins). This document defines every record the demo uses, how it is
> stored, the generation constraints, and the anti-leakage rules. The demo has
> no backend: data ships as static, seeded JSON fixtures, and the client data
> layer derives everything else at runtime.

---

## 1. Storage decision

**Format: static JSON fixtures, generated by a seeded script, checked into the
project.** JSON because the demo is frontend-only, fixtures load with zero
infrastructure, they diff cleanly in git, and typed decoders can validate them
at the boundary exactly as a real API response would be validated.

| File | Content | Grain | Budget |
|---|---|---|---|
| `data/trial.json` | The active trial and its candidate country codes | 1 record | 2 KB |
| `data/countries.json` | Reference data: code, name | 1 per country | 3 KB |
| `data/observations.json` | Site-level historical performance, the atomic grain, and the windowed grid's dataset | 1 per site per source trial | 2 MB max |
| `data/predictions.json` | Model predictions per country for this trial | 1 per country | 5 KB |
| `data/ranking-variables.json` | The default weight vector | 1 per variable | 2 KB |

**Runtime state is never in fixtures.** Selection, edited weights, and finding
statuses live in `localStorage`; the investigation state lives in the URL
(spec §G.3). Fixtures are immutable at runtime.

**Derived data is never stored.** Country rows, ranks, quartiles, and buckets
are computed by the client data layer from `observations.json` on demand. One
grain, one truth-source (BL10).

## 2. Volumes

Sized so the windowed evidence grid is honest: thousands of records, loaded
after first paint, still instant on static hosting.

- 1 trial, 36 candidate countries.
- 12 to 40 sites per country, about 900 sites total.
- 4 to 7 observations per site (one per source historical trial), roughly
  5,000 to 8,000 observations, under 2 MB raw (about 250 KB gzip transfer).
- 12 source historical trials, roughly half flagged benchmark.
- 5 default ranking variables.

## 3. Record shapes

### 3.1 `trial.json`

```
{
  "id": "trial-001",
  "name": "Phase III oncology study",
  "candidateCountries": ["ARG", "AUS", "BEL", "..."]
}
```

### 3.2 `countries.json`

```
[ { "code": "ARG", "name": "Argentina" }, ... ]
```

### 3.3 `observations.json` (the grain)

One record: how one site performed in one source historical trial.

```
{
  "id": "obs-0001",
  "siteId": "site-ARG-01",
  "countryCode": "ARG",
  "sourceTrialId": "src-03",
  "benchmark": true,
  "enrollmentRatePSM": 0.42,        // patients per site per month, achieved
  "targetEnrollmentRatePSM": 0.55,  // planned for that source trial
  "startupDays": 148,               // site activation to first patient
  "investigatorIds": ["inv-ARG-014", "inv-ARG-022"]
}
```

### 3.4 `predictions.json`

Generated independently of the observations (leakage rule L2). One record per
country, scoped to this trial.

```
{
  "countryCode": "ARG",
  "predictedEnrollmentRatePSM": 0.51,
  "predictedStartupDays": 132
}
```

### 3.5 `ranking-variables.json`

```
{
  "id": "rv-01",
  "name": "historical-enrollment",
  "title": "Historical enrollment rate",
  "metricKey": "historicalMedianEnrollmentRate",
  "weight": 0.2,                    // fraction; all weights sum to 1
  "isDefault": true,
  "varType": "Numeric",
  "contribution": "Direct"          // Inverse for lower-is-better metrics
}
```

Five defaults, 0.2 each: historical enrollment (Direct), performance ratio
(Direct), startup time (Inverse), site-to-site variability (Inverse),
predicted enrollment (Direct).

## 4. Derivation rules (computed at runtime, never stored)

For a provenance scope p in {all, benchmark, non-benchmark}, `obs(p)` filters
observations by the `benchmark` flag; `all` is the union.

| Country field (spec §G.1) | Derivation from `obs(p)` |
|---|---|
| `totalSites` | distinct `siteId` |
| `totalInvestigators` | distinct `investigatorId` |
| `multiTrialInvestigators` | investigators present in more than one `sourceTrialId` |
| `historicalMedianEnrollmentRate` | median of `enrollmentRatePSM` |
| `performanceRatio` | median of `enrollmentRatePSM / targetEnrollmentRatePSM` |
| `medianStartupTime` | median of `startupDays` |
| `siteToSiteVariability` | per-site mean enrollment first, then IQR across sites divided by the median across sites |
| `predictedEnrollmentRate`, `predictedStartupTime` | read from `predictions.json`, never derived |
| distribution quartiles (§G.2) | over per-site mean enrollment values |
| distribution buckets (§G.2) | histogram of `startupDays`; bucketSize 100 for days, re-bucket on unit switch |

**Keyset pages (the grid contract, §G.2).** The data layer serves the
evidence grid in cursor pages: stable sort by (field, id) with id as the
tiebreak, cursor = the last row's (sortValue, id), `limit` rows per page,
`nextCursor` null at the end. Offsets are never used, so page cost does not
grow with depth.

**Rank (BL1).** Computed over provenance `all` and the full candidate set, so
human filters never change a country's rank. For each ranking variable:
min-max normalize the metric across candidate countries to [0,1]; if
contribution is Inverse, use 1 minus the normalized value. Composite = sum of
weight times normalized value. Rank = dense rank of the composite, descending;
ties break alphabetically by `countryCode` for determinism.

## 5. Constraints (the generator must satisfy, the validator must check)

| ID | Constraint |
|---|---|
| C1 | All ids unique and lexicographically sortable (they are the keyset tiebreak); every `countryCode` exists in `countries.json`; every `siteId` embeds its country and never appears under two countries; every country in `candidateCountries` has observations |
| C2 | `enrollmentRatePSM` and `targetEnrollmentRatePSM` in (0, 3]; `startupDays` integer in [30, 400] |
| C3 | Every country has at least 5 sites, at least 1 benchmark and 1 non-benchmark observation |
| C4 | Weights are fractions summing to 1.0 (tolerance 1e-9); every `metricKey` maps to a derivable field |
| C5 | Derived quartiles always order: min <= q1 <= median <= q3 <= max |
| C6 | Predictions positive and inside plausibility bounds (enrollment in (0, 3], startup in [30, 400]); bounds are the only allowed relation to observations |
| C7 | No selection, rank, status, or any runtime state in any fixture |
| C8 | No derived field (medians, ratios, quartiles, ranks) stored in any fixture |
| C9 | Same seed produces byte-identical files (deterministic generator) |
| C10 | Total fixture size under 2.5 MB raw; observations between 4,000 and 12,000 records, enough that windowing is honest, small enough to stay instant |

## 6. Anti-leakage rules

Leakage here means: the data secretly contains its own answers, so the demo
proves nothing. Five rules prevent it.

| ID | Rule |
|---|---|
| L1 | **No answer keys.** Planted scenarios (section 7) carry no flag, label, or naming hint in any fixture. Atlas must find them from the metrics alone |
| L2 | **Predictions are independent.** Generated from each country's latent profile plus independent noise, never computed from the sampled observations. Otherwise "prediction far from history" is circular |
| L3 | **Aggregates are never stored** next to the grain. A stored median plus stored raw records is two truth-sources that drift (BL10) |
| L4 | **Rank is never stored.** It exists only as a function of weights and rows (BL1); a stored rank goes stale the moment Ana steers |
| L5 | **Generator latents stay in the generator.** The per-country quality profiles that drive sampling are not exported to any fixture |
| L6 | **Provenance partitions.** `all` = benchmark + non-benchmark, nothing else; no observation carries both or neither |

## 7. Planted scenarios (documented here, invisible in the data)

The generator shapes the latent profiles so the §C agent checks have something
real to find. Documented for humans; per L1, nothing in the fixtures marks them.

| Scenario | Construction | The §C check it exercises |
|---|---|---|
| S1 single-weight carrier | One country with exceptional startup time but mediocre everything else; top 5 by composite, drops 6+ places when weights are equalized | "Is the rank carried by a single weight?" |
| S2 prediction far from history | One country's predicted enrollment about twice its historical median | "Prediction far from its own history" |
| S3 provenance flip | One country strong in non-benchmark sources, weak in benchmark; top 5 reorders under benchmark-only | "Does the top 5 flip under benchmark-only?" |
| S4 median hiding spread | One country with a good median and site-to-site variability above 0.8 | "Good median hiding a wide spread" |
| S5 weights drift | Runtime, not data: triggered when Ana's saved weights deviate far from defaults | "Weights drifted far from defaults" |

## 8. Verification protocol

1. **Generate a sample** with a fixed seed (small: 8 countries) before
   committing to full volumes.
2. **Run the validator**: every C-rule as an assertion, plus adherence checks
   that each record parses against the shapes in section 3 with no extra keys.
3. **Leakage audit**: grep fixtures for any field outside the declared shapes
   (L1, L5); recompute predictions correlation against historical medians and
   require it stays below 0.9 (an independent model correlates, it does not
   copy); confirm no derived values appear in files (L3, L4).
4. **Scenario probe**: run the derivations and confirm S1 to S4 are detectable
   from derived data alone (each check fires on its target country, and on no
   more than two others).
5. Only then scale to full volumes; C9 keeps the result reproducible.

## 9. Sample verification run (executed)

A seeded sample (seed 42, 8 countries, 179 observations, 51 KB) was generated
and validated. Every check passed:

```
PASS  C1   ids unique, referential integrity
PASS  C2   ranges
PASS  C3   >=5 sites, both provenances per country
PASS  C4   weights sum 1, metricKeys derivable
PASS  C5   quartile ordering, all provenances
PASS  C6   prediction plausibility
PASS  C7+C8  no extra keys, no runtime or derived fields in fixtures
PASS  C9   byte-identical files across two runs, same seed
PASS  C10  179 obs, 51 KB total
PASS  L2   prediction/history correlation r = 0.45 (< 0.9)
PASS  S1   plant base rank #5, drops 3 places without the startup weight
PASS  S2   plant prediction/history = 2.51, no other country >= 1.8
PASS  S3   top 3 reorders under benchmark-only; plant moves #1 to #4
PASS  S4   plant variability 0.89, no other country > 0.8
RESULT: 13/13 checks passed (+ C9)
```

Two generated records, verbatim:

```
{ "id": "obs-0001", "siteId": "site-ARG-01", "countryCode": "ARG",
  "sourceTrialId": "src-01", "benchmark": false,
  "enrollmentRatePSM": 0.726, "targetEnrollmentRatePSM": 0.743,
  "startupDays": 138, "investigatorIds": ["inv-ARG-012", "inv-ARG-020"] }

{ "countryCode": "ARG", "predictedEnrollmentRatePSM": 0.625,
  "predictedStartupDays": 132 }
```

Notes from the run, binding for the full-volume generator:
- The sample used 8 countries and 179 observations to prove the constraints;
  the section 2 volumes (36 countries, thousands of observations) supersede it.
  Re-run this validator at full volume during scaffolding; C9 keeps it
  reproducible.
- Planted latents need margin: the S1 country required a clearly bottom-third
  enrollment and a clearly best-in-set startup before the sensitivity probe
  fired; the S4 country needed strongly bimodal site quality. Weak plants
  disappear under min-max normalization.
- The scenario probes double as the acceptance tests for Atlas's checks: the
  same derivations that validated the data are the ones Atlas runs in Flow C.
- The generator and validator ship with the project so the fixtures can be
  regenerated and re-verified at any volume.
